nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sandy and muddy substrates in tropical and subtropical coastal waters; found on sand flats, seagrass beds, and reef margins where it anchors its foot into the substrate

decorPreferences: deep sand bed (5+ cm) for foot burial
open sandy area away from rockwork
no corals or other anemones within 30 cm

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; also an active predator. Generally advised to offer meaty marine foods — chunks of fresh or frozen fish, uncooked shrimp, silversides, or other meaty items — approximately once per week. Larger or rapidly growing specimens may benefit from more frequent feeding. Food should be placed directly onto the oral disc and tentacles will draw it in. Overfeeding can stress the anemone.

avoidWith: most fish not listed as natural clownfish hosts — high risk of being eaten
corals placed near the anemone's territory
slow-moving or bottom-dwelling fish (blennies, gobies, dragonets, etc.)
invertebrates that may wander into contact with tentacles

summary: Haddon's Carpet Anemone is one of the largest anemones kept in reef aquariums, capable of reaching nearly a meter across. Its stinging tentacles are potent enough to capture fish, making tank-mate selection critical. Several clownfish species associate naturally with this anemone and are generally safe with it.
careNotes:
Haddon's Carpet Anemone (Stichodactyla haddoni) is one of the largest and most dangerous anemones commonly available in the reef hobby. Its disc can reach 50–91 cm in diameter in captivity, and its tentacles carry an exceptionally potent sting — among the most powerful of any aquarium anemone. This species has been documented causing severe skin reactions in humans; always use gloves when handling.

Key care requirements:

• Substrate: This is a sand-dwelling species and must be able to bury its foot (pedal disc) into the substrate. A sand bed of at least 5 cm depth is strongly recommended; deeper (7–10 cm) is better and more natural. Do not place it directly on bare rock or glass.

• Size and tank footprint: Adult specimens have been reported to reach up to 91 cm in diameter. The 300 L minimum (approximately 79 US gallons) assumes moderate adult size; larger tanks of 400–600 L are widely advised by experienced reef keepers for full-sized adults. A wide footprint matters more than depth.

• Lighting: High-intensity lighting is required to sustain its zooxanthellae (the photosynthetic algae living within the anemone's tissues that provide much of its nutrition). Placement in the lower sand zone means the light must penetrate the water column adequately — generally 150–350 PAR (a measure of usable light intensity) at the anemone's location is advised.

• Aggression / fish safety: This species has been documented capturing and consuming fish that make contact with its tentacles — including small reef fish, blennies, gobies, dragonets, and other slow or bottom-dwelling species. Only its natural and captivity-established clownfish host species (listed in goodWith) are generally safe in the same aquarium, and even those can occasionally be consumed if they are not of a natural symbiont species. All other fish should be considered at serious risk.

• Spacing from other invertebrates: Generally advised to maintain at least 25–30 cm of clearance between this anemone and any corals or other anemones. The anemone tends to wander when conditions are unsuitable and can devastate adjacent corals.

• Movement: A wandering anemone is typically a sign of unsuitable conditions (lighting, flow, or water chemistry). Once settled in a good location it tends to stay put. A wandering specimen can be sucked into powerheads — intake guards on all pumps are essential.

• Water quality: Requires a well-established reef system. Temperature, salinity, and pH should remain stable. Alkalinity (dKH) is generally advised in the 8–12 range; calcium and magnesium should be maintained at standard reef levels (calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1400 ppm) though this anemone is not a calcifying organism.

• Acclimation: Carpet anemones are sensitive to shipping stress and should be drip-acclimated over 1–2 hours before introduction. They may not fully open for several days post-acclimation.

• Color variants: Available in green, brown, gray, blue (rare), and metallic green color forms. The blue and metallic green variants are particularly sought-after; all share the same care requirements.

• Human handling note: The sting of S. haddoni has been documented as capable of causing severe localized pain, swelling, and in rare cases systemic reactions in humans. Thick gloves and long-sleeved protection are strongly advised when working in a tank housing this species.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Haddon's Carpet Anemone has not been reported in the hobby literature. In the wild, reproduction occurs via both sexual (broadcast spawning) and asexual (pedal laceration) methods. Pedal laceration — where small portions of the foot break off and develop into new individuals — is occasionally observed in captivity, but intentional propagation is not practiced.

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Haddon's Carpet Anemone, Blue (color variant, cross-check)
additional.notes: Cross-checked all water parameters, care level, tank size, lighting, and flow. All parameters identical to the primary listing. Confirms temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, care level Difficult (advanced), min tank 30 gal, lighting High, flow Medium to Strong.

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Haddon's Carpet Anemone, Metallic Green (color variant, cross-check)
additional.notes: Third LiveAquaria variant. Parameters agree on temperature, pH, dKH, care level. Salinity listed as 1.020–1.025 (vs 1.023–1.025 on the standard listing); averaged across all three listings to set lower bound at 1.020. Adult size confirmed as up to 36 inches (91 cm).

additional.name: Wikipedia – Stichodactyla haddoni
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Stichodactylidae, Order: Actiniaria), native range confirmation (Indo-Pacific from Mauritius to Fiji, Ryukyu Islands to Australia), adult size (50–80 cm oral disc diameter), and wild clownfish host species list. Size range: Wikipedia gives 50–80 cm (folded disc); LiveAquaria gives up to 91 cm (36 inches, fully extended). Both values retained: min=50 (Wikipedia), max=91 (LiveAquaria hobbyist data). Minimum tank size of 300 L chosen conservatively — LiveAquaria states 30 gal (114 L) which is clearly insufficient for a species reaching 50–91 cm across; specialist hobby consensus recommends 100+ gallons.
